speaker: interviewer
text: You were hiding in various Christian apartments. And then your mother had an idea. And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your idea? What-- what was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There had been a lady with whom I had spent the summer, a few weeks in the summer, in France. They wanted to take children from the city and send them to the countryside during summer vacation. So as a little child, I had spent some nice summers with her. And I remembered her. And I asked my mom if she knew her address, and maybe contact her, and put me there. And that maybe she would be able to-- on her own, it would be easier to maneuver. So she did that. And so she-- I just have to backtrack a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was such a sweet, quiet, devoted, lovely, remarkable woman. And as a child, she used to drag me from one social welfare to, to another. And some were Jewish and some were non-Jewish. And I used to whine a lot. We had to walk. We were poor. We couldn't afford the bus or the subway. But because she did that, I owe my life to her. And so when I was put in this foster home, and later on in other homes or places, these social welfare agencies-- Jewish as well as non-Jewish, they work together as teams-- they paid for my board and keep, and they looked after me. And they saved my life. And I'm thankful [SOBBING] to my mother. [CRYING] She, she had a lot of foresight. So I stayed with this woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this woman's name, and where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Madame Meurtabes, and she was in a little town maybe 80 miles from Paris. I don't know exactly. She was a widow. Her son was a prisoner of war. And at the time-- at the time that-- when there was no war and I had spent the summer there for a few weeks, it worked out well. But when I went there to stay, it was hell. I, I became an abused child. And so what happened is I got news. My mother wrote to me that she had been arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, your mother wrote that to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She kept in touch with me. And her plan had been to go to the free zone. France was divided into a free and occupied zone. [FRENCH]. And she was caught. She was caught. And I found out later on from one of her friends after the war that she and her friend were planning to go together to, to the free zone. And on that day, [SOBBING] her girlfriend said, I just have a feeling it's not going to work out. I just have an omen about it. Let's do it another time. And my mother said, no. That-- the dies are cast, I'm going. So my mother went alone. And so she was caught. And we don't know if-- you know, in the movie, they show those refugees, they latest one. But they don't know. Those refugees desperately tried to get to the border, Switzerland or the free zone. And the guide turns them in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to this day, we don't know if the guide turned her in, turned them in, or if it just was-- just things went wrong. And so my mother wrote to me that she had been arrested. I've always had a, a-- well, I guess, like a thought in my soul about wrong decisions, you know? She should have listened to her girlfriend. Her girlfriend, later on, went by herself and made it. Was that the last time you heard from your mother, when you were still in this country house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have a very moving story to say, to tell you. And it's very painful. And that is-- the last time I heard from my mother, she sent me a package from the concentration camp in Paris. She was in the region of Bordeaux. And it was a weird package. It was addressed to me. And when I opened the package, it had a chocolate bar that was melted. It had a rotten pear, and it had a piece of bread that was stale.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this lady, Madame Meurtabes, broke into a rage. And she says, your mother's absolutely crazy. Who would think of sending her daughter such a package? And she took the whole package and she threw it in the fire. And I, I couldn't say or do anything. But I remember it very vividly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, she had second thoughts about it. She says, there must be something about that package. So she thew the bread out of the fire. And she sliced the bread. And in the bread was money. My mother had put the money for me. But of course, she got hold of it. And her, her eyes just was shining with joy, just getting that money. I, I have to mention that I was abused in that-- this woman, because of her son being a prisoner of war, and being a widow, and, and maybe not having enough money, she would beat the shit out of me. She had a big stick. And if I coughed and disturbed her sleep, or-- she would beat me up. And I was also starved. And she always threatened to denounce me to the Gestapo. And she would sometimes say, if I didn't have to hide you, I could have a nice hunk of ham or salami. And if the check from the social welfare agency came late, she would beat me severely. And so I was cold and hungry, and I was beaten up. And one time, we had coupons to get bread at the local bakery in the village, and I lost the money and the coupon that she gave me. And I came in and told her, and she beat me till I almost passed unconscious. And she said, don't come back till you get the bread. You find a way. So when I went back to the bakery, and he saw I was all beaten up and in tears, he gave me loaf of bread. And I also want to mention, it's kind of funny, but we all have ways of earning a living. She had the field. And so it was a big deal to pick up the dung from the horse, or, or mules, or donkeys that went down the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to go after school, and I'd run after the horses, donkeys, and mules. And I'd pick-- with my bare hand-- I'd pick up the dung, and put it in the bag.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had a decent meal that day. She was very happy when I did that. So I learned to make a living early in life to pay for my room and board. And I was very fortunate. The rural school teacher was a husband and wife team, and a gentleman from the Red Cross. I would-- I never mentioned that I was Jewish, but they, they knew things. And so one day, a social worker came and took me out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of this woman's home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of this woman's home. And I all-- I'm glad, because I always had terrible feeling that she would turn me in, because in those days, people who turned in Jews were highly rewarded with ham, and salami, and money, and opportunities to move up in society, and so on. So this is depicted in the movie, [FRENCH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at this school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I, I do want to go back about my mother in the [? Klarsfeld ?] Papers. And that is, my mother was arrested-- according to the [? Klarsfeld ?] Papers-- my mother was arrested and delivered to the Gestapo in the most inhuman condition. [FRENCH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So even before the Gestapo got to her, the French collaborator-- La Milice, I think they called them-- had got hold of her and all the other victims and had delivered her and them in the most inhuman condition. So the French delivered your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The French Milice, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Um, just going back a little bit again to this-- to this woman, were you-- were there any other children in this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a young boy who was put there by his parents for a brief time, and he-- it was put-- his parents would send him packages, and would visit him. And he eventually left. I never saw him again. He, he came briefly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this, this school teacher and her husband, who realized that you were Jewish, did you, by the way--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if they realized. We never talked about it, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: What name did you go under? Did you have a false name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at, at the time, no. Not to my knowledge. There may have been names on papers that I was not aware.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you went to school while you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they call you? Did they call you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They called me Maya.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the school teacher and the wife-- the husband, what did they do with you? What did you do next? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: My next stop, I was put in the children's-- it looked like a, a big children's home, big foster home. We were all Jewish children. I can't tell you the exact number, but it seems that maybe there were 30 or 40. I remember a large number. We were waiting for something else to happen. And when I look at my life in those days, I called myself a suitcase ready to be picked up. That's the way I felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were a group of children there. And we-- it was in Montmorency, a little town outside of Paris, known for cherry trees. And it was a brief stay, maybe a month or two.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the-- a social worker came and picked me up and took me in Paris to the convent of Notre Dame de Sion, Notre Dame of Zion. And we stayed in the servant quarters, maybe 10 days. I don't-- and there were a few Jewish girls. And I-- there-- I-- there were three of us. There probably were more, but I remember we were three. There was the oldest one, Lilian. Me-- Lilian was about 13, 14. I was 10, 11. And the youngest one was Elizabeth, and she was about six. And we were there. It was a beautiful, beautiful convent. I understand that de Gaulle sent his children there. A lot of wealthy children who wanted-- wealthy people who wanted their children to be well educated in an aristocratic fashion, knowing music, arts, literature, and manners, were sent there. It was an exclusive school. Absolutely gorgeous, with gorgeous grounds. Castle-like, manor-like. Antique furniture, absolutely gorgeous. But we were in the servant quarters. I remember the young girls and older women always ironing, and washing, and mending, and dusting, and washing the floor. Was buzzing activity all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this, this social worker that brought you to this place, was she Jewish, not Jewish? Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well, after we stayed in the-- after we stayed in, in the convent in Paris a few days, the three of us, Lilian, myself, and Elizabeth, we were taken to the railway station in Paris and sent to Grenoble. And, and I was told later on-- I mean, were talking 50 years later-- one of the nuns, whose name was Deniz Pola Aguanish, she said-- I just found out 50 years later-- she said she took us to the railway station in Paris, and she was in charge of putting children in safe hiding places. So in the train, there were two social workers. Now, if my recollections are correct, one was Colette and the other one was Ethel. And they told us-- now, again, this is from what I remember. There were two social workers, very young girls. One said she was 16, and the other one was 18. And they told us that they were Jewish, but they had a baptismal certificate, and they passed as Catholics. And that we must never tell anyone that we're Jewish. And-- [CRYING] --they were going to put us in a safe place. And we would do whatever we were told to do, and what all the other children did. We would participate in all activities, and that they would keep in touch with us. And that they would write and come and visit. And they did. They kept their words.

speaker: interviewer
text: This, this was in the convent in Grenoble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we stayed a few days in the convent of Notre Dame de Sion of Grenoble, and then we were put in the convent of Les Soeurs du Bon Pasteur, The Sisters of the Good Shepherd.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was-- it was-- what do you call that? The nuns don't go out. It was a cloister. The nuns all wore white, and they were-- so that's where we were. And we stayed there till the eve of the liberation of Grenoble. Describe a day in, in, in the convent, in the-- in the one that you stayed for the duration?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd like, if I may, I'd like to describe a day and a holiday, because that-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely. Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The nuns were very nice to us, but there were hundreds and hundreds of children. I'd like to mention that half of the-- what seemed to be like an orphanage, some of the children were delinquent, or they were abandoned by parents who were criminals. And so it was like, like a juvenile hall, and it was like a jail. And because of it, because of government looking after them, they were well fed. And some of those children, they had brother and sister in our part. We were in the part of [FRENCH] abandoned children, and, as I said, some of the children had brother and sister in the other side. They had visiting days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very austere. It was very strict. The rules were very strict. We usually got up at 4:00 or 5:00 AM, and the region of Grenoble was freezing. I remember just freezing weather.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my hands and feet were covered with blisters. And when I pulled the glove or the socks, part of the flesh went with it. And we were hungry all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got up at 4:00 or 5:00 AM, and we went to Mass in the chapel. And I always liked the Mass, because I was in the children's choir. I liked the music. And the chapel was pretty, and it was warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it-- and, and enjoyed it. I enjoyed the whole Mass liturgy. It was in Latin. I remember talking to a nun, and she said, you must have faith. So I've always thought about that word, avoir la foi. Well, so they would serve us-- after Mass, they would serve us a breakfast, which we couldn't eat. It had, like, potato peels and half-cooked carrots. We, despite our hunger, we were unable to eat it. So most of us went without breakfast. Lunch was a slice of bread. And sometimes it was two boiled potatoes with one or two teaspoons of jam. And sometimes, instead of two boiled potatoes, we had a bowl of beans or a bowl of noodles. And there were sometimes cooked, mixed carrots, cooked boiled carrots with vinegar. And we'd eat it, but it tasted horrible. And then at-- we would have a nap. We did go to school. We had morning classes, and we had afternoon classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was-- just to-- this was Jewish and non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were part of the-- we part of the Catholic community in that convent. It was a city-- it was a city or a village in itself, because they had huge field. They-- the nuns cultivated the fields, and they baked their bread. And they-- oh, they had factories going on there. The older girls would, would sew and embroider, trousseau for wealthy women, gorgeous silk wedding dresses. I mean, we would see them sewing. They had magic hands. And then they also had a glove factory. And some of the children, even the little ones, we would just cut the threads off. We'd do that maybe an hour or two after class, and we enjoyed it. You know, it was a way to keep busy. We often knitted. And-- yeah. I had learned knitting from my mom. And I would knit, and when I got through with the wool, I'd undo it and start knitting all over again. And if you had asked me what I was knitting I didn't quite know what I was-- maybe a little shawl, or I learned to do gloves and socks. And we would sew, and the nun would say, oh, your stitches are too big. You gotta make invisible stitches. So I never learned to, to master the art of sewing or embroidery. We had some reading. All the books were censored by the nuns. And the social worker sent us a couple books which I never got. So apparently it wasn't-- it didn't pass the rules. At four o'clock, we had bread. A slice of bread. And I think it was our last bread for the day. And so many of us would hide the bread [SOBBING] under the pillow so we could have a little breakfast. And sometimes, by the time you went to the bathroom or washed, that slice of bread was gone. And then, in the evening, the dinner was pretty much like lunch, two boiled potato with one or two teaspoon of jam, or replaced by a bowl of beans or noodles. Now, girls who were menstruating got an extra portion of noodles and-- oh. Kids who were sick got an extra portion, too. So a lot of us wanted to be sick. I never got to be sick. I might have a cold and sneeze, but my temperature never went up. And Lilian, she was a-- she was a troublemaker from the day go. She got in bed, and she moved the thermometer. It was a rectal thermometer. And she moved it-- you could rub it-- she moved it so high that she'd be dead by now. And the nuns caught on. And boy, she, she no longer was considered-- you know, she no longer got her extra portion of noodles.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked about a holiday that you wanted to discuss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Anyway, we went to school. I just want to mention that it, it had a little school home with two floors. One had, maybe, like kindergarten through, through fifth and sixth grade. And the other had seventh grade and up. And it was so freezing, once in awhile we would get up from our chair and go like this, and jump all over to keep warm. We were always hungry, always freezing. And when we went to bed-- we had a nap and the nuns would say, you have a nap, and you won't think of hunger. But I remember always dreaming of food and how hungry I was. It was a period of starvation. Now, the holidays were wonderful. We changed uniforms. We had bright uniforms. And we had a good breakfast, and I think a little meat and a little milk. And the festivities, lots of songs and drama plays. And just a feeling of joy. And I have to mention that this was a particular Christmas that I had my doll with me. My mother had given me a doll. And it was pretty beat up by then, too. And people would say, you're a big girl, you still like dolls. But I-- that doll was like a baby blanket. And, and my doll was nowhere in sight, and I was crying and looking for my doll. And on Christmas day, at the breakfast table, there was a package. [SOBBING] And when I opened it, it was-- my doll was in it with a whole brand new outfit. The girls and the nun had made the-- a beautiful wardrobe. You know, with underwear and funny, cute little hat, and dress, and outfit. And I was overjoyed. So that was a surprise. I also need to mention that when I got to meet Deniz 50 years later and I was on her list, there were 150 children on that list that she--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Deniz was the head of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: The head of? Just describe again. Deniz was the head nun?

speaker: interviewee
text: She, she-- Deniz was the woman responsible for saving my life. She was the head of the French Underground, who was in charge of rescuing Jewish children, as well as countless thousands of refugees. Well, on that list that I found myself in, there were 150 Jewish children. And she said that that list of 150 Jewish children, they were asking people for toys for, for us at the time of Christmas. In those days, everybody celebrated Christmas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of us, 150 children, got to receive a, a special Christmas toy on that day, in those dark moments. And she said, we never knew where the money was coming from that people would donate, and some of the money came from London. During this time that you were in this convent, do you ever remember Germans coming to look at it? Do you ever remember moments of danger where you might--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were told-- from what-- the way the nuns talked-- and we were told that we were in a very strat-- strategic, I don't know how you pronounce the word--

speaker: interviewer
text: Strategic.

speaker: interviewee
text: --strategic position, because nearby was the headquarter of the Gestapo. And the other side nearby was the headquarter of the French resistance, the Underground. And then, as the liberation came near, we had the Americans drop-- and Allies dropping bombs all over us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it got to be-- incidentally, the nuns would called the Allies that we-- and the resistance as the terrorists. That's the way the Germans called them, the terrorists. So as the liberation was approaching, we lost track of time. And that is, we would be awakened at two o'clock in the morning, four o'clock. We lost track of time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our day was completely dislocated. We would go to sleep with our clothes on. And, and while the bombs were falling all of us, and the shooting was going on, we would have our arms like this in the cross, praying Christ to spare us. And I understand a lot of people in the convent were killed, children and nuns. And occasionally, we'd go by the body and pay respect to body. There were quite a few funerals that took place. And a lot of children died from illness, loneliness, salvation. These were the children, quote, 'unwanted.' Some of them nobody ever came to see them. The one thing I learned, because I, I think at that moment being Jewish wasn't such a thing I-- with me. I mean, it was something we were going to keep it a secret and never mention. But I was aware of the suffering [SOBBING] of all the children in there. All of us. All of us were hungry, and lonely, and starving, and afraid. And no parents. You know, we were on the mercy of whoever took care of us. And in a sense, I was very fortunate, because the, the older children took care of the younger one all the way down. So to some degree, there was affection and caring going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're coming to the end of this tape, so we'll continue on tape number three.
